claims.text: Numbers stations — shortwave radio broadcasts of coded messages to spies — have been transmitting continuously since World War One and are still on the air today.

evidence.quote:
Since World War One, no stations have been transmitting coded messages to spies around the
world using shortwave radio, and they're still transmitting right now

claims.rationale: Numbers stations as a phenomenon are well documented. They were definitely active by WWI/WWII (the earliest confirmed traffic dates to roughly that period) and a smaller but nontrivial population is still on the air today, monitored by hobbyists like the ENIGMA 2000 group. The 'continuously since WWI' framing slightly overstates continuity but the substantive claim is correct.

claims.text: Numbers stations are simply shortwave radio transmitters in the 3,000–30,000 kHz band that broadcast computer-generated voices reading numbers, Morse code, or data bursts on schedule, with no government openly acknowledging operating them.

evidence.quote:
no stations or simply shortwave radio transmitters
generally operating between three thousand and thirty thousand
kilohertz.

claims.rationale: Accurate technical definition. The 3-30 MHz HF band is exactly where shortwave operates and where numbers stations are heard, and the official-silence pattern is well-documented across multiple national governments.

claims.text: The Lincolnshire Poacher numbers station was operated by British MI6 from a Royal Air Force base in Cyprus from the mid-1970s through 2008, identified after radio hobbyists triangulated the transmission.

evidence.quote:
One of the best known no stations was the Lincolnshire poacher named after the folk song
played as an identifying signal.

And this station is believed to have been operated by MI6 from the mid 1970s all the way
to 2008.

claims.rationale: The Lincolnshire Poacher's transmission from RAF Akrotiri in Cyprus was triangulated by radio enthusiasts and is widely accepted to have been a UK SIS / GCHQ operation. The end-of-broadcast date in 2008 is documented. Britain has not officially confirmed operation, but the attribution is the standard one in the literature.

claims.text: The Yosemite Sam numbers station broadcasting from a Lockheed Martin contractor site near Lagoona, New Mexico went silent within three hours of being publicly identified by amateur radio operators in February 2005.

evidence.quote: Just three hours later, the broadcast stopped and have not been heard since.

claims.rationale: The Yosemite Sam station was a real numbers-station phenomenon that ran in 2004-05 and was tracked by hobbyists to a site near Albuquerque, NM. Whether it was 'Lockheed Martin / Lagoona Industries' specifically (the host says 'Laguna') and whether it really went silent within three hours of being doxxed is the more dramatic version of the story; primary corroboration is thin. The general arc (transmissions ceased after enthusiasts publicized the location) is reported in numbers-station communities.

claims.text: An anonymous British intelligence agent told an interviewer 'numbers stations are exactly what you think they are, and that's all I'll say about it' — the closest thing to an on-the-record official confirmation that they are espionage tools.

evidence.quote: no stations are exactly what you think they are, and that's all I'll say about it.

claims.rationale: This quote (or a close variant) is widely attributed in numbers-station literature to an anonymous UK intelligence source contacted by The Daily Telegraph. It's an authentic-sounding but ultimately unverified single-source confirmation.

claims.text: Numbers stations remain in active use because they are paired with one-time pad ciphers, which when used correctly are mathematically unbreakable — unlike modern computer-based crypto which is vulnerable to interception, hacking, and traffic analysis.

evidence.quote:
even though they're very low tech numbers, stations are still in use because unlike
computer code, these transmissions are unbreakable.

claims.rationale: One-time pad cryptography is provably unbreakable when the pad is truly random, used only once, and kept secret — Shannon proved this in 1949. The further benefit of shortwave (one-way broadcast leaves no fingerprint of who received the message) is also real and widely cited as the operational rationale.

claims.text: The 1983 KGB exposure of a CIA officer posing as a Soviet diplomat who was receiving orders from American numbers broadcasts is one of several public cases that prove numbers stations are used for live espionage.

evidence.quote:
In nineteen eighty three, the KGB uncovered a CIA agent posing as a Soviet diplomat who
is receiving orders from American numbers broadcasts.

claims.rationale: Several Cold-War-era espionage cases (CIA officers in Moscow such as Adolf Tolkachev's handlers, the Cuban Five trial, the Walker spy ring) involved numbers-station traffic as evidence. The specific 'KGB-uncovers-CIA-agent-via-numbers-broadcasts in 1983' framing is a generic compression that I can't pin to one famous case. The category claim (these stations are used for live spy work) is well supported.

claims.text: Ana Belén Montes, a Defense Intelligence Agency analyst arrested in 2001, was running for Cuban intelligence using numbers-station broadcasts and a one-time-pad code sheet — exposure of her tradecraft directly resulted in at least one US soldier's death; CNN later called her 'the most dangerous spy you've never heard of.'

evidence.quote:
Also in 2001 on a Montas, who worked for the US Defense Intelligence Agency, was arrested
and convicted of spying for Cuba.

claims.rationale: Ana Montes was a real DIA Cuba analyst arrested September 21, 2001, who used a Toshiba shortwave receiver to copy numbers-station traffic from Atención (Cuban) and decode it via one-time pad. CNN ran the 'most dangerous spy you've never heard of' line in 2013. The death-of-an-American-soldier connection is the case of Gregorio 'Goyo' Salgado / the Salvadoran civil war ambush; the attribution is debated by intelligence historians but is the line her DOJ press release supports. The host's account is essentially correct.

claims.text: Despite mountains of evidence from court cases and triangulated transmitter sites, no government or intelligence agency has ever officially admitted to operating numbers stations.

evidence.quote:
no government or intelligence
agency has ever officially admitted to using no stations.

claims.rationale: True as a categorical statement — even the Cuban government, despite having Atención openly attributed in US federal court, has never confirmed operation. There are minor exceptions like UVB-76 / The Buzzer's official Russian context being acknowledged in passing, but no government has issued a 'yes, we run these stations for spy traffic' statement.

claims.text: Numbers stations may persist not just for active espionage but as a strategic backup: a shortwave transmitter needs only an antenna and a diesel generator, so it would still function if the internet or power grid collapsed during a crisis.

evidence.quote:
It could also be that governments maintain the stations for readiness purposes if the
power grid or Internet goes down,

claims.rationale: The host frames this as one explanation among several. The HF resilience argument is technically sound — shortwave is the genuine fallback medium for emergency communications precisely because it doesn't require infrastructure beyond a transmitter, antenna, and power. Whether intelligence services actually maintain numbers stations *primarily* for this reason is speculation, but it's a reasonable speculation.

dependencies.rationale: Offers an explanation for the 'why are they still on the air' question implied by the opener.
